Moreover, on Tiny-ImageNet with a ResNet-50 backbone, it reduces overall training time by 29% with only 0.05 GiB extra VRAM. These results show that DT-SupCon is a simple and effective drop-in enhancement for supervised contrastive learning.

item.title: MGA-CLIP: A Multigranularity Attribution Framework for Cross-Modal Explainability in CLIP
item.dc:creator: Xiaotian Cheng
Tianyi Zhou
Siwen Yin
Hao Zhang
Wei Xiong
Weiping Ding
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
item.description: The contrastive language–image pretraining (CLIP) model has demonstrated remarkable performance in multimodal tasks, but the interpretability of its similarity-based cross-modal alignment mechanism has attracted considerable attention. However, existing visual explanation methods typically rely on single-gradient information, which is easily affected by noise and activation saturation, resulting in unstable and rough interpretations. To address this issue, we propose a multigranularity attribution framework for CLIP (MGA-CLIP) that provides a systematic analysis of the internal learning behaviors of deep multimodal neural networks. At the coarse-grained level, the method adopts the concept of integrated gradients (IGs) to construct an integration path from a baseline to the input, accumulating path gradients to obtain a stable gradient tensor, which is then used to weight the feature space for a robust global interpretation. At the fine-grained level, the method leverages the model’s cross-modal alignment characteristics and its internal attention dependencies to quantify the semantic relevance between visual patches and text embeddings, thereby capturing localized embedding interactions. Unlike previous works that treat global and local cues independently, our framework establishes an explicit linkage between channel-level global attributions and patch-level semantic reasoning, enabling consistent explanations across different granularity levels. By fusing the coarse- and fine-grained results, MGA-CLIP generates semantically consistent and cross-modally aligned explanations. Moreover, we employ the proposed attribution framework in a text-based adversarial patch experiment, demonstrating its strong capability to reveal the internal reasoning behavior of the CLIP and to provide insightful interpretability References for analyzing multimodal alignment mechanisms. Extensive experiments show that MGA-CLIP significantly outperforms existing methods in attribution stabil... item.title: Occlusion-aware multi-modal 3D object detection via multi-stage cross-modal fusion
item.dc:creator: Weihua Wang
Peng Zhang
Haonan Ding
Dawei Pi
Guodong Yin
item.description: 3D object detection is a core task in autonomous driving perception systems, where LiDAR provides precise geometric structure information and cameras contribute rich semantic features. However, existing multi-modal detection frameworks often struggle to balance accuracy, robustness, and real-time performance in scenarios with sparse point clouds, insufficient semantics, and high computational overhead. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a novel multi-modal 3D object detection framework based on multi-stage cross-modal fusion. Specifically, the LiDAR branch integrates lightweight pillar encoding with BEV transformation and leverages a region proposal network (RPN) to efficiently extract spatial ROIs while preserving detailed geometric information. The image branch extends the YOLO network to 3D detection, enabling unified 3D bounding box prediction within a common coordinate system. In the fusion stage, an ROI-Attention module is designed, where the Translation-Aware Module (TAM) achieves cross-modal geometric alignment and the Region-Aware Module (RAM) explicitly mines complementary semantic and geometric features. The resulting unified feature volume is then fed into a lightweight detection head for classification and 3D bounding box regression. We validate the proposed method on both the KITTI dataset, the nuScenes dataset and real-vehicle experiments. Results demonstrate that the framework significantly improves detection accuracy and robustness while maintaining real-time performance, offering clear advantages in detecting small, occluded, and distant objects, and exhibiting strong adaptability in complex autonomous driving scenarios. Published: 2026-05-30T06:31:07+00:00 Venue: Image and Vision Computing Score: 0.784 (must_read)

3D object detection is a core task in autonomous driving perception systems, where LiDAR provides precise geometric structure information and cameras contribute rich semantic features. However, existing multi-modal detection frameworks often struggle to balance accuracy, robustness, and real-time performance in scenarios with sparse point clouds, insufficient semantics, and high computational overhead. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a novel multi-modal 3D object detection framework based on multi-stage cross-modal fusion. Specifically, the LiDAR branch integrates lightweight pillar encoding with BEV transformation and leverages a region proposal network (RPN) to efficiently extract spatial ROIs while preserving detailed geometric information. The image branch extends the YOLO network to 3D detection, enabling unified 3D bounding box prediction within a common coordinate system. In the fusion stage, an ROI-Attention module is designed, where the Translation-Aware Module (TAM) achieves cross-modal geometric alignment and the Region-Aware Module (RAM) explicitly mines complementary semantic and geometric features. The resulting unified feature volume is then fed into a lightweight detection head for classification and 3D bounding box regression. We validate the proposed method on both the KITTI dataset, the nuScenes dataset and real-vehicle experiments. Results demonstrate that the framework significantly improves detection accuracy and robustness while maintaining real-time performance, offering clear advantages in detecting small, occluded, and distant objects, and exhibiting strong adaptability in complex autonomous driving scenarios.

item.title: Attribute-calibrated local embeddings for prompt learning in vision-language models
item.dc:creator: Zhongchen Ma
Jiahao Song
Xingchen Wu
Yintong Wang
Lijian Gao
item.description: Parameter-efficient prompt learning is an effective strategy for adapting Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to downstream tasks. However, most existing methods rely solely on a single global image representation, overlooking valuable fine-grained details. Because standard VLM pre-training primarily aligns text with global embeddings, raw patch-level features within the vision encoder are often noisy and lack local discriminative power. To address this limitation, we introduce ACLE-Prompt, a novel framework that generates Attribute-Calibrated Local Embeddings (ACLE) to enrich local representations for prompt learning. Rather than directly using isolated and noisy patches, ACLE-Prompt employs a dynamic memory bank that aggregates high-quality, cross-sample patch exemplars from a frozen vision encoder. For each input image, a sparse multi-head cross-attention mechanism retrieves relevant exemplars from this memory to calibrate and refine the input image’s local patch features. We further adopt a synergistic dual-branch framework, where a global branch maintains holistic alignment and a local branch learns prompts directly from these calibrated embeddings. Extensive experiments on 11 benchmarks show that ACLE-Prompt achieves a new state-of-the-art average 16-shot accuracy of 84.3%, outperforming the existing methods and exhibits strong robustness in fine-grained classification, few-shot learning, and domain generalization. Published: 2026-05-30T05:21:33+00:00 Venue: Information Sciences Score: 0.784 (must_read)

Journal: Information Sciences

Relevance: 0.784 (must_read)

Parameter-efficient prompt learning is an effective strategy for adapting Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to downstream tasks. However, most existing methods rely solely on a single global image representation, overlooking valuable fine-grained details. Because standard VLM pre-training primarily aligns text with global embeddings, raw patch-level features within the vision encoder are often noisy and lack local discriminative power. To address this limitation, we introduce ACLE-Prompt, a novel framework that generates Attribute-Calibrated Local Embeddings (ACLE) to enrich local representations for prompt learning. Rather than directly using isolated and noisy patches, ACLE-Prompt employs a dynamic memory bank that aggregates high-quality, cross-sample patch exemplars from a frozen vision encoder. For each input image, a sparse multi-head cross-attention mechanism retrieves relevant exemplars from this memory to calibrate and refine the input image’s local patch features. We further adopt a synergistic dual-branch framework, where a global branch maintains holistic alignment and a local branch learns prompts directly from these calibrated embeddings. Extensive experiments on 11 benchmarks show that ACLE-Prompt achieves a new state-of-the-art average 16-shot accuracy of 84.3%, outperforming the existing methods and exhibits strong robustness in fine-grained classification, few-shot learning, and domain generalization.

item.title: Frequency-Aware Cosine Similarity Alignment Network in Remote Sensing Semantic Segmentation
item.dc:creator: Fulin He
Zhiyong Lv
Cheng Shi
Jiake Li
Jón Atli Benediktsson
item.description: We propose an innovative frequency-domain alignment method to address feature shift issues encountered by the Segment Anything Model (SAM) in remote sensing image segmentation. Existing SAM-based models often struggle to capture semantic discrepancies across domains due to inherent differences in imaging mechanisms between natural and remote sensing images, as well as significant scale variations. To address this limitation, we introduce a Cosine Similarity Gated Smoothing (CSGS) module built upon features extracted from the pre-trained SAM, incorporating a semantic consistency constraint. Specifically, cosine similarity is employed to quantify semantic differences between heterogeneous features corresponding to the same object, while a multi-directional graph-based propagation strategy refines the semantic information flow. Furthermore, a Deformable Cross-Scale Attention Bridge (DCAB) is designed to adaptively sample low-level local features and perform weighted fusion via a temperature-controlled gating mechanism, thereby improving segmentation performance in fine-detail regions. In addition, a Multi-Scale Frequency Domain Adapter (MSFA) is proposed to improve cross-domain feature learning by decoupling and integrating low-frequency components, effectively mitigating domain discrepancies. The code is publicly available at: https://github.com/CHINASCBZ/FreqAlign . Published: 2026-05-29T15:19:52+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.782 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Fulin He; Zhiyong Lv; Cheng Shi; Jiake Li; Jón Atli Benediktsson

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.133054

Relevance: 0.782 (must_read)

We propose an innovative frequency-domain alignment method to address feature shift issues encountered by the Segment Anything Model (SAM) in remote sensing image segmentation. Existing SAM-based models often struggle to capture semantic discrepancies across domains due to inherent differences in imaging mechanisms between natural and remote sensing images, as well as significant scale variations. To address this limitation, we introduce a Cosine Similarity Gated Smoothing (CSGS) module built upon features extracted from the pre-trained SAM, incorporating a semantic consistency constraint. Specifically, cosine similarity is employed to quantify semantic differences between heterogeneous features corresponding to the same object, while a multi-directional graph-based propagation strategy refines the semantic information flow. Furthermore, a Deformable Cross-Scale Attention Bridge (DCAB) is designed to adaptively sample low-level local features and perform weighted fusion via a temperature-controlled gating mechanism, thereby improving segmentation performance in fine-detail regions. In addition, a Multi-Scale Frequency Domain Adapter (MSFA) is proposed to improve cross-domain feature learning by decoupling and integrating low-frequency components, effectively mitigating domain discrepancies. The code is publicly available at: https://github.com/CHINASCBZ/FreqAlign .

item.title: Towards comprehensive multi-task land cover change detection leveraging vision-language model and LLM-driven agents
item.dc:creator: Tengxi Wang
Yiru Wang
Shuai Zhang
Yuxuan Liang
Wufan Zhao
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Land cover change detection is essential for assessing the impacts of urbanization on ecological systems and economic development. As urbanization accelerates, land cover changes involve both semantic transitions and structural modifications, substantially increasing the complexity of detection tasks. However, existing research has primarily focused on 2D change detection, with limited integration of multi-dimensional and multi-modal data. This limitation has restricted the effective modeling of inter-class transitions and spatial structural evolution. Moreover, most methods lack end-to-end pipelines adapted to real-world scenarios, constraining their capacity for comprehensive representation and quantitative impact assessment. To address these challenges, we introduce UrbanChange, the first dataset designed for multi-task (2D and 3D) land cover change detection. It integrates semantic and structural change maps from multi-sensor data with fine-grained change captions, providing a comprehensive foundation for evaluating and analyzing land cover transformations. Leveraging UrbanChange, we develop a vision–language model that directly infers multi-dimensional changes from optical imagery. We further develop GaiaAgent by incorporating this model as a core perception module, forming the agentic workflow for land cover change detection and analysis. GaiaAgent coordinates five task-specific agents in an automated pipeline for instruction interpretation, task decomposition, detection, and quantitative analysis. A Human-in-the-Loop mechanism is incorporated to produce interactive, interpretable reports, enhancing robustness in complex scenarios. Extensive experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of GaiaAgent. On the UrbanChange dataset, GaiaAgent achieves a 3.9% improvement in F1 and a 0.35 m reduction in cRMSE compared with the strongest baseline. In downstream applications across multiple analytical scenarios, GaiaAgent demonstrates improved reliability and adaptability in land cover change detection and interpretation. Published: 2026-05-29T13:51:41+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.782 (must_read)

Land cover change detection is essential for assessing the impacts of urbanization on ecological systems and economic development. As urbanization accelerates, land cover changes involve both semantic transitions and structural modifications, substantially increasing the complexity of detection tasks. However, existing research has primarily focused on 2D change detection, with limited integration of multi-dimensional and multi-modal data. This limitation has restricted the effective modeling of inter-class transitions and spatial structural evolution. Moreover, most methods lack end-to-end pipelines adapted to real-world scenarios, constraining their capacity for comprehensive representation and quantitative impact assessment. To address these challenges, we introduce UrbanChange, the first dataset designed for multi-task (2D and 3D) land cover change detection. It integrates semantic and structural change maps from multi-sensor data with fine-grained change captions, providing a comprehensive foundation for evaluating and analyzing land cover transformations. Leveraging UrbanChange, we develop a vision–language model that directly infers multi-dimensional changes from optical imagery. We further develop GaiaAgent by incorporating this model as a core perception module, forming the agentic workflow for land cover change detection and analysis. GaiaAgent coordinates five task-specific agents in an automated pipeline for instruction interpretation, task decomposition, detection, and quantitative analysis. A Human-in-the-Loop mechanism is incorporated to produce interactive, interpretable reports, enhancing robustness in complex scenarios. Extensive experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of GaiaAgent. On the UrbanChange dataset, GaiaAgent achieves a 3.9% improvement in F1 and a 0.35 m reduction in cRMSE compared with the strongest baseline. In downstream applications across multiple analytical scenarios, GaiaAgent demonstrates improved reliability and adaptability in land cover change detection and interpretation.

item.title: Improving 3D Gaussian Splatting Compression by Scene-Adaptive Lattice Vector Quantization
item.dc:creator: Hao Xu
Xiaolin Wu
Xi Zhang
item.description: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) is rapidly gaining popularity for its photorealistic rendering quality and real-time performance, but it generates massive amounts of data. Hence compressing 3DGS data is necessary for the cost effectiveness of 3DGS models. Recently, several anchor-based neural compression methods have been proposed, achieving good 3DGS compression performance. However, they all rely on uniform scalar quantization (USQ) due to its simplicity. A tantalizing question is whether more sophisticated quantizers can improve the current 3DGS compression methods with very little extra overhead and minimal change to the system. The answer is yes by replacing USQ with lattice vector quantization (LVQ). To better capture scene-specific characteristics, we optimize the lattice basis for each scene, improving LVQ’s adaptability and R-D efficiency. This scene-adaptive LVQ (SALVQ) strikes a balance between the R-D efficiency of vector quantization and the low complexity of USQ. SALVQ can be seamlessly integrated into existing 3DGS compression architectures, enhancing their R-D performance with minimal modifications and computational overhead. Moreover, by scaling the lattice basis vectors, SALVQ can dynamically adjust lattice density, enabling a single model to accommodate multiple bit rate targets. This flexibility eliminates the need to train separate models for different compression levels, significantly reducing training time and memory consumption. Published: 2026-05-29T19:58:30+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.781 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Hao Xu; Xiaolin Wu; Xi Zhang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3696119

Relevance: 0.781 (must_read)

3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) is rapidly gaining popularity for its photorealistic rendering quality and real-time performance, but it generates massive amounts of data. Hence compressing 3DGS data is necessary for the cost effectiveness of 3DGS models. Recently, several anchor-based neural compression methods have been proposed, achieving good 3DGS compression performance. However, they all rely on uniform scalar quantization (USQ) due to its simplicity. A tantalizing question is whether more sophisticated quantizers can improve the current 3DGS compression methods with very little extra overhead and minimal change to the system. The answer is yes by replacing USQ with lattice vector quantization (LVQ). To better capture scene-specific characteristics, we optimize the lattice basis for each scene, improving LVQ’s adaptability and R-D efficiency. This scene-adaptive LVQ (SALVQ) strikes a balance between the R-D efficiency of vector quantization and the low complexity of USQ. SALVQ can be seamlessly integrated into existing 3DGS compression architectures, enhancing their R-D performance with minimal modifications and computational overhead. Moreover, by scaling the lattice basis vectors, SALVQ can dynamically adjust lattice density, enabling a single model to accommodate multiple bit rate targets. This flexibility eliminates the need to train separate models for different compression levels, significantly reducing training time and memory consumption.

item.title: Towards operational automated greenhouse gas plume detection and delineation
item.dc:creator: Brian D. Bue
Jake H. Lee
Andrew K. Thorpe
Philip G. Brodrick
Daniel Cusworth
Alana Ayasse
Vassiliki Mancoridis
Anagha Satish
Shujun Xiong
Riley Duren
item.description: Operational deployment of a fully automated facility-scale greenhouse gas (GHG) plume detection system remains challenging for fine spatial resolution imaging spectrometers, despite recent advances in deep learning approaches. With the dramatic increase in data availability, however, automation continues to grow in importance for emissions monitoring. This work reviews and addresses several key obstacles in the field: data and label quality control, prevention of spatiotemporal biases, and correctly aligned modeling objectives. We demonstrate through rigorous experiments using multicampaign data from airborne and spaceborne instruments that convolutional neural networks (CNNs) are able to achieve operational detection performance when these obstacles are alleviated. We demonstrate that a multitask model that learns both instance detection and pixelwise segmentation simultaneously can successfully lead towards an operational pathway. We evaluate the model’s plume detectability across emission source types and regions, identifying thresholds for operational deployment. Finally, we provide analysis-ready data, models, and source code for reproducibility, and work to define a set of best practices and validation standards to facilitate future contributions to the field. Published: 2026-05-29T16:25:23+00:00 Venue: Remote Sensing of Environment Score: 0.781 (must_read)

Authors: Brian D. Bue; Jake H. Lee; Andrew K. Thorpe; Philip G. Brodrick; Daniel Cusworth; Alana Ayasse; Vassiliki Mancoridis; Anagha Satish; Shujun Xiong; Riley Duren

Journal: Remote Sensing of Environment

DOI: 10.1016/j.rse.2026.115506

Relevance: 0.781 (must_read)

Operational deployment of a fully automated facility-scale greenhouse gas (GHG) plume detection system remains challenging for fine spatial resolution imaging spectrometers, despite recent advances in deep learning approaches. With the dramatic increase in data availability, however, automation continues to grow in importance for emissions monitoring. This work reviews and addresses several key obstacles in the field: data and label quality control, prevention of spatiotemporal biases, and correctly aligned modeling objectives. We demonstrate through rigorous experiments using multicampaign data from airborne and spaceborne instruments that convolutional neural networks (CNNs) are able to achieve operational detection performance when these obstacles are alleviated. We demonstrate that a multitask model that learns both instance detection and pixelwise segmentation simultaneously can successfully lead towards an operational pathway. We evaluate the model’s plume detectability across emission source types and regions, identifying thresholds for operational deployment. Finally, we provide analysis-ready data, models, and source code for reproducibility, and work to define a set of best practices and validation standards to facilitate future contributions to the field.

item.title: Harmonize task divergence with MUNet: Bridging semantic gaps between segmentation and classification for medical images
item.dc:creator: Junwei Yang
Laquan Li
Weisheng Li
Shenhai Zheng
item.description: Segmentation and classification tasks in medical image processing are often closely related. However, their collaborative optimization within a unified model often faces challenges such as inter-task feature redundancy, objective conflicts, and semantic gaps. To reconcile this task discrepancy, this study proposes an efficient multi-task learning network named MUNet. This method comprises four synergistic innovative modules: the Multi-View Feature Modulator, the Multi-Scale Evolution Generator, the Multi-Task Bottleneck Layer, and the Consistency-Guided Upsampling Module. Specifically, the Multi-View Feature Modulator learns a set of complementary features through diversity constraints. Building upon this, the Multi-Scale Evolution Generator facilitates the fusion and evolution of multi-level features, enhancing inter-task synergy. The Multi-Task Bottleneck Layer refines task-specific semantic features from the shared representations. Meanwhile, the Consistency-Guided Upsampling Module guides the recovery of resolution by computing semantic consistency between deep and shallow features, effectively bridging the semantic gap across different feature levels. Experimental results on multiple medical image datasets demonstrate that MUNet can simultaneously optimize both segmentation and classification tasks through the collaborative operation of these four modules. (The code will be public at https://github.com/JOJO-Fly/MUNet after acceptance). Published: 2026-05-30T15:24:00+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.780 (must_read)

Journal: Pattern Recognition

Relevance: 0.780 (must_read)

Segmentation and classification tasks in medical image processing are often closely related. However, their collaborative optimization within a unified model often faces challenges such as inter-task feature redundancy, objective conflicts, and semantic gaps. To reconcile this task discrepancy, this study proposes an efficient multi-task learning network named MUNet. This method comprises four synergistic innovative modules: the Multi-View Feature Modulator, the Multi-Scale Evolution Generator, the Multi-Task Bottleneck Layer, and the Consistency-Guided Upsampling Module. Specifically, the Multi-View Feature Modulator learns a set of complementary features through diversity constraints. Building upon this, the Multi-Scale Evolution Generator facilitates the fusion and evolution of multi-level features, enhancing inter-task synergy. The Multi-Task Bottleneck Layer refines task-specific semantic features from the shared representations. Meanwhile, the Consistency-Guided Upsampling Module guides the recovery of resolution by computing semantic consistency between deep and shallow features, effectively bridging the semantic gap across different feature levels. Experimental results on multiple medical image datasets demonstrate that MUNet can simultaneously optimize both segmentation and classification tasks through the collaborative operation of these four modules. (The code will be public at https://github.com/JOJO-Fly/MUNet after acceptance).

item.title: FLORO: A Multimodal Geospatial Foundation Model for Ecological Remote Sensing Across Sensors and Scales
item.dc:creator: Jorge L. Rodriguez
Victor Angulo Morales
Areej Alwahas
Mariana Elias Lara
Fida Mohammad Thoker
Kasper Johansen
Bernard Ghanem
Fernando T. Maestre
Matthew F. McCabe
item.description: Foundation models offer a promising route to transferable remote sensing representations, but many current approaches depend on very large pretraining datasets and fixed sensor configurations, limiting their suitability for ecological and environmental applications, where observations often vary across platforms, spatial and spectral resolutions, and available modalities. We introduce FLORO, a multimodal geospatial foundation model designed to learn transferable representations from a small but highly diverse remote sensing corpus. FLORO is pretrained using masked autoencoding on a heterogeneous combination of Sentinel-1, Sentinel-2, SkySAT imagery, elevation, and UAV-derived data. To accommodate sensor variability, FLORO incorporates availability-aware inputs that indicate which spectral bands and auxiliary modalities are present in each sample, enabling a unified input space across heterogeneous sensor configurations. We evaluated FLORO on the PANGAEA benchmark under a frozen-encoder protocol across scene classification, segmentation, and regression tasks. Despite being pretrained on a smaller corpus than competing foundation models, FLORO achieved strong and stable transfer across optical, optical-SAR, and optical-elevation benchmarks spanning medium-resolution satellite, airborne, and ultra-high-resolution UAV imagery. FLORO obtained the second-best average segmentation performance across six PANGAEA benchmarks, trailing only a recently introduced foundation model pretrained on over two orders of magnitude more images, remained competitive on scene classification, and was robust in regression tasks, while qualitative results showed improved preservation of spatial structure in flood, urban, biomass, and canopy-height prediction settings. In a separate controlled experiment on EuroSAT-MS, geo-positional encoding further improved classification relative to absolute positional encoding. Published: 2026-05-27T08:55:54+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.779 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jorge L. Rodriguez; Victor Angulo Morales; Areej Alwahas; Mariana Elias Lara; Fida Mohammad Thoker; Kasper Johansen; Bernard Ghanem; Fernando T. Maestre; Matthew F. McCabe

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.779 (must_read)

Foundation models offer a promising route to transferable remote sensing representations, but many current approaches depend on very large pretraining datasets and fixed sensor configurations, limiting their suitability for ecological and environmental applications, where observations often vary across platforms, spatial and spectral resolutions, and available modalities. We introduce FLORO, a multimodal geospatial foundation model designed to learn transferable representations from a small but highly diverse remote sensing corpus. FLORO is pretrained using masked autoencoding on a heterogeneous combination of Sentinel-1, Sentinel-2, SkySAT imagery, elevation, and UAV-derived data. To accommodate sensor variability, FLORO incorporates availability-aware inputs that indicate which spectral bands and auxiliary modalities are present in each sample, enabling a unified input space across heterogeneous sensor configurations. We evaluated FLORO on the PANGAEA benchmark under a frozen-encoder protocol across scene classification, segmentation, and regression tasks. Despite being pretrained on a smaller corpus than competing foundation models, FLORO achieved strong and stable transfer across optical, optical-SAR, and optical-elevation benchmarks spanning medium-resolution satellite, airborne, and ultra-high-resolution UAV imagery. FLORO obtained the second-best average segmentation performance across six PANGAEA benchmarks, trailing only a recently introduced foundation model pretrained on over two orders of magnitude more images, remained competitive on scene classification, and was robust in regression tasks, while qualitative results showed improved preservation of spatial structure in flood, urban, biomass, and canopy-height prediction settings. In a separate controlled experiment on EuroSAT-MS, geo-positional encoding further improved classification relative to absolute positional encoding.
